summary: Jeff Atwood examines the failed effort to enforce well-formed XHTML on the web, citing Ian Hickson's study showing over 93% of tested HTML files contained syntax errors. Even Tim Berners-Lee acknowledged that the push for strict XML compliance didn't succeed because browsers never enforced it. Atwood argues that HTML's tolerance of malformed markup is a feature, not a bug—browsers aren't compilers and shouldn't fail on invalid code. The post concludes with a pragmatic, if provocative, question about whether developers should care about HTML validity when neither browsers nor users do.
key_points: A study of 667,416 HTML files found over 93% had syntax errors, suggesting malformed HTML is the norm rather than the exception
The XHTML transition failed because browsers didn't enforce well-formedness, so the HTML-generating public never moved
Even Google's homepage contained 63 HTML validation errors at the time of writing
HTML's tolerance of errors is intentional by design—browsers parse tag soup rather than failing like compilers
Showing constant 'this page is broken' warnings would be bad UX since almost every page would trigger them
money_quotes: Over 93%. That's only syntax errors in the HTML, not checking the CSS, the content types, the semantic errors.
The large HTML-generating public did not move, largely because the browsers didn't complain.
Like it or not, we live in a world of malformed HTML.
Browsers aren't compilers. They don't fail spectacularly when they encounter invalid markup. And nor should they.
HTML is, and always has been, tolerant by design.
We'll always be awash in a sea of tag soup.
Your browser doesn't care if your HTML is well-formed. Your users don't care if your HTML is well-formed. So why should you?
key_insight: The web's tolerance of malformed HTML is a pragmatic reality that won, and standards purists lost, because browsers chose user experience over strict enforcement.
